steps.title: Use Text 2's history
steps.content: Text 1 describes purists who want bluegrass to exclude mainstream influences and who treat Bill Monroe's 1940s recordings as the standard for the genre. Text 2 challenges that reasoning by explaining that Monroe's original bluegrass sound was not purely an extension of Southern folk music. According to Text 2, Monroe created bluegrass by combining folk with commercial genres that had appeared only a few decades earlier, including jazz and blues.

steps.title: Reject unsupported criticisms
steps.content: Choice A speculates about what other genres purists personally enjoy, which Text 2 never discusses. Choice B focuses on whether purists can enforce their preferences, but enforcement is not addressed. Choice C claims the issue is enlarging bluegrass's audience, while Text 2 argues from the genre's mixed origins, not audience size. Because purists use Monroe's sound as a reason to reject mainstream influences while overlooking that Monroe's sound already included such outside influences, Text 2 would view their perspective as illogical. The answer is D.
